english: pas (pas) filled, impregnated with bile Med.; lus sems dga-bdés k͟yáb-par gyúr-nas (lus sems dga' bdes khyab par gyur nas) body and soul (filled with) full of joy Glr.; bar k͟yáb-pa (bar khyab pa) to fill up an intermediate space; to make (a country etc.) full of light, religion, happiness, frq.; tams-ćád-la drìn-gyis (thams cad la drin gyis) to embrace all creatures with benevolence; kùn-la k͟yab-pa (kun la khyab pa) in grammar: capable of being joined to any word, comprising all of them, Glr.; k͟yab-čé-ba (khyab che ba) comprehensive; used also in the way of censure: everywhere and nowhere, to be met with everywhere Mil.; k͟yab-yḍál (khyab gdal) or rdól (rdol) comprehensive, extensive. — k͟yab (khyab) seems also to be a sbst. in k͟yab-čé-ba (khyab che ba), and still more so in rgya bod yoṅs k͟yáb-tu grágs-pa-la (rgya bod yongs khyab tu grags pa la) according to what is spoken in the whole compass of India and Tibet Tar. 87.

Vishnu, a Brahmanical divinity, appearing, like Brahma and Shiva, also in Buddhist legends, yet principally known in his quality as gza-sgra-gćan-dzin (gza' sgra gcan 'dzin) (Rāhula), conqueror of the demon that threatens to devour sun and moon; hence k͟yab-júg-gzér (khyab 'jug gzer) Med., *k͟yab-júg-gi nad* (khyab 'jug gi nad) W., *ra-hu-lẹ ne* (ra hu le ne) C., epilepsy.

Cs., Sch. yard, court-yard, Cs. also gallery. It is, like tsoms (tshoms), a space that is to be found in many Tibetan houses, and may be compared to the compluvium of the Romans, being open in the middle, and on the sides generally enclosed by verandas. k͟yams (khyams) may therefore be called court-yard, when it is on the same level with the ground, (so also perh. Tar. 89, 4, reading k͟yams-su (khyams su) for K͟yams-su (khyams su)); but in the upper stories such a construction is unknown in European architecture. k͟yams-stód (khyams stod) the upper court-yard, k͟yams-smád (khyams smad) the lower one; k͟yams-tóṅs (khyams stongs) Cs.: 'impluvium'.
Cs.: p. n. = kams (khams), v. kams (khams) 3.

wylie: khyams pa, khyar pa, khyal pa

english: dog, k͟yi rmug (khyi rmug) B. and C.; the dog bites, W.: barks; *so tab* (so btab) W.; bites; *tam* (tham) W. lays hold of; k͟yi bós-nas ma brduṅ (khyi bos nas ma brdung) proverb: if you call the dog, then you must not beat him Glr. — k͟yi rkaṅ-gnyís (khyi rkang gnyis) Sch. 'a bastard dog, a cur' (?) — k͟yi-skád (khyi skad) the barking. — k͟yi-kán (khyi khang) dog-kennel, — k͟yi-gu (khyi gu) a puppy. — k͟yi-rgán (khyi rgan) an old dog. — k͟yi-rnó (khyi rngo) the itch of dogs. — k͟yi-dám (khyi dam) 'dog's seal', a mark burnt in; stigma C., W. — k͟yi-dúg (khyi dug) poison of hydrophobia Sch. — k͟yi-mdúd-pa (khyi mdud pa) the pairing of dogs Sch. — k͟yi-pul (khyi phul) dog-kennel, dog-house. — *k͟yi-pal-jór* (khyi pal jor) W. Blitum virgatum. — k͟yi-spyáṅ (khyi spyang) W. jackal. — k͟yi-pó (khyi pho) a male dog. — k͟yi-brú (khyi bru) Sch. a vicious, biting dog. — k͟yi-sbráṅ (khyi sbrang) dog's fly. — k͟yi-mo (khyi mo) a female dog, bitch. — k͟yi-smyón (khyi smyon) canine madnes, hydrophobia C., W.; also mad dog = k͟yi smyón-pa (khyi smyon pa). — k͟yi-rdzi (khyi rdzi) dog-keeper. — k͟yi-gźóṅ (khyi gzhong) trough for dogs and other animals, manger. — k͟yi-śig (khyi shig) flea.
1. v. k͟yi (khyi). — 2. W. bud (of leaves and branches, not of blossoms), eye (of a plant).

chase, hunting, esp. of single huntsmen, not of a party; stable-stand, cf. liṅs (lings); *k͟yi-ra-la čá-če* (khyi ra la ca ce) W. to go a hunting, *k͟yi-ra čo-če* (khyi ra co ce), *gyáb-če* (rgyab ce), *gyúg-če* (rgyug ce) id.; *k͟yi-ra-la čá-kan* (khyi ra la ca mkhan) hunter, sportsman; k͟yi-ra-ba (khyi ra ba) B. and C., *k͟yi-ra-pa* (khyi ra pa) W. huntsman.
a sort of treacle made of seṅ-ldeṅ (seng ldeng) Wdn.
breadth of the hand with the thumb extended, a span.

1. house, not as a building, but as a dwelling-place of man, a home. Even when in Sik. they speak of *śiṅ-k͟yim* (shing khyim), *nyúg-k͟yim* (smyug khyim) a house of wood, of bamboo, the idea of habitation, dwelling-place predominates in these expressions. k͟yim-na (khyim na) at home, k͟yim-du (khyim du) home (to go home); k͟yim daṅ k͟yim-na (khyim dang khyim na) house for house, each in his house Tar. 151. 22; k͟yim spó-ba (khyim spo ba) to remove to another place; k͟yim skyóṅ-ba (khyim skyong ba) to have a house-
